_comment: Copy to projects.json to run several projects from one bot process.
Each entry claims a Discord guild (server), and optionally specific channels
within it. Anything unclaimed gets NO response — the bot never falls back to
another project's configuration.
Delete this file (or don't create projects.json) to stay in single-project
mode, where everything comes from environment variables.
Anything you omit falls back to the matching environment variable, so shared
settings like githubToken and gitAuthorEmail can stay in .env.
ROUTING — how a message finds its repo:
1. A project whose channelIds include the message's channel wins.
2. Otherwise the guild's default project — the entry with no channelIds.
3. Otherwise no response.
That is how one server works on several repos: give each extra repo its own
channels and leave the main one as the default. A message always resolves to
exactly one project, so no conversation ever spans two checkouts.
A guild may have at most one default, and no two projects may claim the same
channel. Either mistake stops the bot at startup rather than guessing.
channelIds doubles as that project's allowlist, so there's no need to repeat
them in allowedChannelIds.
Each project gets its own checkout under REPO_ROOT (default ./repos) named
after its key, so two projects can never commit into each other's tree.
IDs: enable Developer Mode in Discord, then right-click a server for 'Copy
Server ID' or a channel for 'Copy Channel ID'. Keep them quoted — unquoted
they parse as numbers and lose precision.
TOKENS: a fine-grained GitHub PAT is scoped to specific repositories. One
scoped to a single repo cannot clone the others, and the bot will fail to
prepare them at startup. Either widen the token to cover every repo below,
or give each project its own githubToken.
